parents.father_research_notes: Brazil Pembroke — also enslaved on a Tilghman plantation; took surname 'Pembroke' from his enslaver. Reunited with the family after James's mother's enslaver agreed to purchase him.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mother enslaved by another Tilghman family member; James and his mother given to Frisby Tilghman as a wedding gift when James was four years old.

sources.short: ConnecticutHistory.org — Reverend James Pennington: A Voice for Freedom

notable.notable_blurb: First Black student at Yale; abolitionist minister

education.institution: Yale Divinity School (informally — denied formal admission)
education.notes: Pennington applied to Yale Divinity School and was denied admission due to skin color. Yale allowed him to attend classes without matriculating, but under degrading conditions: he could not speak, ask questions, borrow books, or appear in the catalog. After completing his studies, he was ordained a Congregational minister — Yale implicitly recognizing his education. The first Black student known to have attended Yale University. On April 24, 2023, Yale awarded him a posthumous M.A. Privatim.
First African American to receive an honorary doctorate from a European university. The University of Heidelberg later established the James W.C. Pennington Award in his honor.

career.notes: Worked as coachman for a wealthy lawyer. Paid tutors out of his earnings; taught himself Latin and Greek.
Taught school in Newtown — present-day Elmhurst, Queens. In 1838 he married Frederick Douglass and Anna Murray there.

career.organization: Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church), Hartford CT
career.notes: Pastored the Hartford congregation and taught at a local school for African American children. The church had been organized by African Americans in 1826 after segregated treatment in white churches.

'A Textbook of the Origin and History... of the Colored People' (1841) — considered the first history of African Americans, challenging Thomas Jefferson's arguments about African inferiority. 'The Fugitive Blacksmith' (London, 1849) — his memoir slave-narrative.
Published two anti-slavery newspapers in Hartford for a short period after the decline of the Colored American newspaper.

career.organization: First Colored Presbyterian Church (Shiloh Presbyterian Church), New York City
career.notes: Larger congregation (400+ members); declined initially but eventually accepted. Used position to fund his published memoir and meet national abolition leaders.

career.organization: First Black missionary society — funded the Mende return after the Amistad case
career.notes: Pennington formed the United Missionary Society (which became the American Missionary Society) to raise funds for the Mende captives' return to Sierra Leone after the 1841 Amistad ruling. Two members of his Hartford church, Henry and Tamar Wilson, went with the missionary group.

Helped form a committee against NYC public-transit segregation after schoolteacher Elizabeth Jennings's 1854 arrest. Pennington's own 1859 arrest in a 'white only' streetcar led to a State Supreme Court ruling that racial segregation in NYC streetcars was illegal.
Identified as a pacifist but during the war added his voice to recruiters of African American soldiers, alongside Frederick Douglass.

career.organization: Natchez MS, Portland ME, Jacksonville FL
career.notes: Brief Natchez ministry; three years in Portland ME; appointed by the Presbyterian Church 1870 to organize an African-American congregation in Jacksonville FL where he died.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale's first Black student, denied formal admission
narrative_blocks.body: Pennington applied to Yale Divinity School and was denied admission due to skin color. Yale allowed him to attend classes without matriculating, under degrading conditions — he could not speak, ask questions, borrow books, or appear in the catalog. Yale awarded him a posthumous M.A. Privatim in 2023, nearly two centuries later.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote the first history of African Americans, 1841
narrative_blocks.body: While serving as minister at the Talcott Street Church in Hartford, Pennington published 'A Textbook of the Origin and History... of the Colored People' (1841) — considered the first history of African Americans, drawing on the works of his time. He challenged Thomas Jefferson's arguments about African inferiority and refuted the 'curse of Ham' justification for slavery.

narrative_blocks.header: John Hooker bought his freedom from slavery, 1851
narrative_blocks.body: When the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered Pennington in the North, his Hartford friend and parishioner John Hooker opened secret negotiations with the heirs of his former enslaver Frisby Tilghman. Hooker briefly took legal 'ownership' of Pennington in order to manumit him in New York; British abolitionists in Dunse, Berwickshire raised the purchase funds.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Frederick Douglass in Newtown, Long Island
narrative_blocks.body: While teaching school in Newtown (present-day Elmhurst, Queens), Pennington married Frederick Douglass to Anna Murray in 1838. The couple had no money for the ceremony; he agreed graciously. He later helped found the Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society and the United Missionary Society that funded the Mende return to Sierra Leone after the Amistad case.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker bought his freedom from slavery in 1851
cross_connections.description: When the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered Pennington in the North, John Hooker — his friend and parishioner at the Talcott Street Church in Hartford — opened secret negotiations with Frisby Tilghman's heirs. Hooker briefly took legal 'ownership' of Pennington to manumit him in New York; British abolitionists raised the purchase funds.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST091.primary_name: Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church)
INST091.name: Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church)
INST091.notes:
Founded 1826 by African Americans in Hartford after segregated treatment in white churches. Originally housed at Talcott Street, the congregation later took the name Faith Congregational Church. The church operated a school for African American children in its lower level — Hartford's regular school system technically accepted all children but African American parents established separate schools (1830) due to discriminatory treatment. The school met in the church's two lower rooms.

Rev. James W.C. Pennington pastored the congregation from 1840 to 1847 — writing during this period the first published history of African Americans (1841) and serving as a delegate to the Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society. It was here that John Hooker (H01626) became Pennington's friend and later, in 1851, legally purchased Pennington's freedom from his Maryland enslaver after the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered him.

Other notable members of the period: Deacon James Mars (Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society executive committee); Henry Foster (state temperance society organizer); Ann Plato (teacher and author); Henry and Tamar Wilson (missionaries who accompanied the Mende return to Sierra Leone after the Amistad ruling).

INST121.primary_name: Yale Divinity School (Theological Department of Yale College / 'New Haven Seminary')
INST121.name: Yale Divinity School (Theological Department of Yale College / 'New Haven Seminary')
INST121.notes:
Founded 1822 as the Theological Department of Yale College; informally called 'New Haven Seminary' by 19th century pastors. Renamed Yale Divinity School in 1869.
